figures.caption: Fig. 3: Robotic manipulation over spatially varying frictional surfaces. Left: Environment with three friction patches, µ = 0.8,1.2,1.5. Middle: For a fixed goal, ES lacks a good initial pushing direction, RL fails in the high-friction region, and ES-DRL drives the block toward the goal. Right: For a time-varying goal, RL fails early, whereas ES-DRL tracks the goal.
Fig. 2: Architecture of the ES-DRL controller. A supervisor selects binary β ∈ {0,1} based on the contact flag and combines a = βtaRL t +(1−βt)aES t . ES is initialized from DRL (dotted).

Fig. 1: Training success rates for the Fetch push and pickand-place tasks under DDPG. The dotted gray curves denote the raw success rates, while the solid black curves denote the smoothed training trends. Each training epoch consists of 100 episodes.
Fig. 4: 3D tracking of a time-varying goal. The RL-only controller (top) fails to track the goal, whereas ES-DRL (bottom) maintains substantially closer tracking.
